title: Consistent Evaluation of Operators Involving the Position Operator in the Bloch Representation: Application to the Orbital Moment
authors: Daehyeon An, Junmo Jeon, Se Kwon Kim
abstract: The position operator plays a central role in condensed-matter observables such as velocity, orbital moment, and electric polarization. In solid-state physics, the evaluation of operators incorporating the position operator has not reached a consensus, as observed in the operator-level discrepancy between the local circulation of Wannier functions and the self-rotation of wave packets. Here, to achieve a consistent evaluation of such operators, we propose three rules for evaluating operators involving the position operator in the Bloch representation. The rules are devised to satisfy physical conditions: independence from the choice of unit cell, preservation of Hermitian conjugacy for the product of operators, and recovery of the correct intraband velocity. We further address the gauge dependence of the position operator and introduce a scheme termed gauge filtration, which systematically removes gauge-dependent contributions from the operators containing the position operator. This methodology ensures that the quantities obtained from the operator evaluation correspond to observable physical phenomena. By applying our framework, we reconcile the results concerning the self-rotation of the wave packet and the local circulation of the Wannier function. We expect our proposal to establish a consistent framework for evaluating operators involving the position operator.
summary: This paper develops a comprehensive set of rules to consistently evaluate complex operators containing the position operator ($\hat{r}$) within the Bloch representation. By introducing 'gauge filtration,' the authors resolve long-standing discrepancies between different theoretical methods for calculating physical quantities like the orbital moment in solids. This establishes a robust, gauge-invariant framework essential for accurate condensed matter theory.
main_problem: The central challenge is establishing a mathematically consistent framework for evaluating expectation values of operators involving the position operator ($\hat{r}$) within the Bloch representation in solid-state physics.
main_result: The authors propose three rigorous rules and introduce 'gauge filtration' to systematically remove unphysical gauge dependencies, successfully reconciling different formalisms (self-rotation vs. local circulation) for calculating observables like the orbital moment.
method: The methodology involves developing specific regularization schemes ($\Delta_N$-regularization) and deriving general evaluation rules for operator product chains $\langle \hat{O}\hat{r}\hat{Q} angle$ to ensure physical consistency across different theoretical approaches.
model_or_system: The study is set in the context of infinitely periodic solid-state systems, using Bloch states $|\psi_{nk}angle$ and analyzing observables like velocity, orbital moment ($\hat{m}$), and polarization within the Brillouin zone.
key_observables: Orbital moment ($\hat{\mathbf{m}}$), intraband group velocity ($v_n$), expectation values of $\hat{r}$, and gauge-invariant quantities derived from Berry connection/curvature.
important_parameters: The analysis depends on the lattice structure (e.g., rectangular, orthorhombic) and requires taking limits related to unit cell size and regularization parameters ($N 	o \infty$).
main_assumptions: Key assumptions include assuming spinless, non-degenerate bands; the derived rules are strictly valid only when the maximum derivative order acting on the regularization function is $\le 1$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by identifying inconsistencies (discrepancies between formalisms), proposing three corrective evaluation rules, introducing gauge filtration to remove artifacts from origin shifts/gauge choices, and finally applying this framework to reconcile results for the orbital moment calculation.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter theory rather than AMO systems directly, the rigorous mathematical techniques developed—especially systematic gauge removal and regularization of position operators in periodic boundary conditions—are highly relevant for any theoretical work involving Bloch wavefunctions or Berry curvature calculations in quantum optics or cold atom lattices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
